article.title: Webb Finds a Quenched, Non-Rotating Giant Galaxy When the Universe Was Less Than 2 Billion Years Old
article.summary: A UC Davis-led team using JWST reports XMM-VID1-2075, a galaxy several times the stellar mass of the Milky Way with no detectable rotation, in Nature Astronomy.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A team led by the University of California, Davis has used the James Webb Space Telescope to study a massive early-universe galaxy that shows no sign of rotation, a finding the researchers describe as unexpected for an object that existed when the cosmos was less than 2 billion years old. The galaxy, catalogued as XMM-VID1-2075, already contained several times as many stars as the Milky Way and had stopped forming new ones, according to the team's [UC Davis](https://www.ucdavis.edu/news/non-rotating-early-galaxy-surprise-astronomers) announcement. The study was published in Nature Astronomy on May 4, 2026, as reported by [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-rotating-early-galaxy-astronomers.html).

## What We Know

XMM-VID1-2075 was first flagged by the MAGAZ3NE (Massive Ancient Galaxies at z>3 NEar-Infrared) survey, which combined ground-based spectroscopy from the W. M. Keck Observatory in Hawaiʻi with follow-up imaging and spectroscopy from JWST, according to [UC Davis](https://www.ucdavis.edu/news/non-rotating-early-galaxy-surprise-astronomers). The earlier Keck data established that the galaxy was among the most massive in the early universe and that it was already quenched, meaning it had stopped producing new stars, [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-rotating-early-galaxy-astronomers.html) reports.

With JWST, the team was able to resolve the internal motions of the galaxy's stars. "This one in particular did not show any evidence of rotation, which was surprising and very interesting," lead author Ben Forrest, a research scientist in the UC Davis Department of Physics and Astronomy, told [UC Davis](https://www.ucdavis.edu/news/non-rotating-early-galaxy-surprise-astronomers). According to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260506225135.htm), Forrest described the technique as well established for nearby galaxies but difficult at high redshift: "This type of work has been done a lot with nearby galaxies...but it's very difficult to do with high redshift galaxies because they appear a lot smaller in the sky."

The absence of rotation places XMM-VID1-2075 in the category of "slow rotators," a stellar-dynamics regime usually seen in the giant elliptical galaxies of the present-day universe rather than in objects whose light has been traveling toward Earth for roughly 12 billion years, [Earth.com](https://www.earth.com/news/early-universe-galaxy-breaks-the-rules-by-barely-spinning/) notes. Forrest told [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260506225135.htm) that the kinematic profile is "consistent with some of the most massive galaxies in the local universe, but it was a bit surprising to find it so early on."

## The Collision Hypothesis

In nearby giant ellipticals, the random stellar motion that defines slow rotators is generally interpreted as the accumulated outcome of many mergers over billions of years. XMM-VID1-2075 did not have that time. The team is therefore considering whether a single, large collision could explain the galaxy's dynamics, [UC Davis](https://www.ucdavis.edu/news/non-rotating-early-galaxy-surprise-astronomers) reports.

Forrest pointed to a feature in the JWST imagery as supporting evidence. "For this particular galaxy, we see a large excess of light off to the side. That's suggestive of some other object that has come in and is interacting with the system and potentially changing its dynamics," he told [Earth.com](https://www.earth.com/news/early-universe-galaxy-breaks-the-rules-by-barely-spinning/). [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260506225135.htm) carries a near-identical quote attributing the same off-axis light excess to a possible interacting object.

## What We Don't Know

The single-collision hypothesis is not yet confirmed. The published study presents the kinematic measurement and the suggestive off-axis feature, but the question of whether one counter-rotating encounter is sufficient to erase coherent rotation in a galaxy of this mass remains open. The MAGAZ3NE team observed three quenched, massive early-universe galaxies in this campaign and found a striking heterogeneity, [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-rotating-early-galaxy-astronomers.html) reports: "one is clearly rotating, one is 'kind of messy,' and one has no rotation but a lot of random motion" — with XMM-VID1-2075 falling into the third category.

The MAGAZ3NE collaboration includes researchers at York University, Tufts University, the University of Toronto, UC Riverside, UC Irvine, the W. M. Keck Observatory, Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München, the University of Wisconsin-Madison, Arizona State University, and UC Merced, with funding from NASA, the Space Telescope Science Institute, and the National Science Foundation, according to [UC Davis](https://www.ucdavis.edu/news/non-rotating-early-galaxy-surprise-astronomers).
